mappings.rationaleAI: The emergence of geology covers the development of paleontology and stratigraphy (A.3.a.ii), the Neptunist-Plutonist controversy (A.3.a.iii), and uniformitarianism (A.4.a.iii).

Fossils from early speculations through Mary Anning and William Smith cover speculations about fossils (A.2.a.ii), development of paleontology and stratigraphy (A.3.a.ii), and faunal succession (A.4.a.ii).
Plate tectonics as the unifying theory of Earth science covers the theory of plate tectonics (A.5.a.vi) and is central to the geologic sciences as a discipline (B.2).
Seismology covers advances in geophysics and the study of the internal structure of the Earth (A.5.a.iii) and is a core geologic discipline (B.2).
Ice ages from Agassiz through Milankovitch cycles cover the evidence for an Ice Age (A.4.a.iv) and the study of glacier motion and ice sheets (A.5.b.v).

The geological formation of Britain covers plate tectonics (A.5.a.vi), stratigraphic processes (A.4.a), and demonstrates how Britain's geological diversity made it central to geology as a science (B.2).
Ageing the Earth from Ussher through Kelvin to radiometric dating covers estimates of the age of the Earth (A.4.a.v) and development of radiometric dating (A.5.a.i).
Catastrophism covers Cuvier's fossil evidence for mass extinctions (A.3.a.ii), the debate with uniformitarianism (A.4.a.iii), and the concept of faunal succession (A.4.a.ii).
The Earth's core covers seismological evidence for internal structure, dynamo theory, and core-mantle interactions, all central to advances in geophysics (A.5.a.iii) and the geologic sciences (B.2).

Oceanography from HMS Challenger through modern deep-sea research covers the beginnings of oceanography (A.4.b.ii), 20th-century hydrologic sciences (A.5.b), and the hydrologic sciences as a discipline (B.3).
Vulcanology covers speculations about volcanic eruptions (A.2.a.i), the relationship between volcanism and plate tectonics (A.5.a.vi), and is a core geologic discipline (B.2).
Meteorology from Aristotle through numerical weather prediction covers the study of weather and climate (A.4.c.iii), 20th-century atmospheric sciences (A.5.c), and physical geography (B.1).
Antarctica's ice sheets, climate role, and scientific significance connect to the study of high-latitude ice sheets (A.5.b.v) and the hydrologic sciences including glaciology (B.3).
Water's unique properties including the water cycle and climate regulation connect to the study of water resources (A.5.b.i) and the hydrologic sciences (B.3).
Doggerland's submergence due to post-glacial sea level rise connects to the study of glacier motion and high-latitude ice sheets (A.5.b.v) and geological sciences (B.2).
The Mariana Trench covers charting of ocean floors (A.5.b.iii), subduction and plate tectonics (A.5.a.vi), and oceanography (B.3).
The PETM as a rapid global warming event 56 million years ago connects to advances in paleontology including paleoecology (A.5.a.v) and climate classification (A.5.c.v).
The Permian-Triassic extinction covers advances in paleontology (A.5.a.v), the most severe mass extinction and its implications for understanding ecological collapse.
Extremophiles in deep-sea hydrothermal vents and other extreme environments connect to the study of seawater chemistry and oceanic resources (A.5.b.i, A.5.b.ii) and the hydrologic sciences (B.3).
